unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8 of your Year 8 English course. This unit is called Global Storytelling and Creativity, and it is one of the most exciting units you will explore this year. Throughout this unit, you will discover how stories shape the world around us, how creative people use language to inspire and persuade, and how you can develop your own storytelling skills. You will read a powerful original story called The Edge of the Infinite, which follows a young Somali storyteller named Hannah as she competes in a national youth storytelling competition in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. Her journey will show you that great stories do not simply entertain people; they change lives, preserve memories, and give voice to communities that might otherwise be forgotten.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners developing global storytelling and creative craft

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A planned series of actions carried out over time to achieve an important goal.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The students launched a campaign to raise money for their school library.
practiceSentences: The librarian led a tireless campaign to bring mobile book vans to the distant villages.
How did one letter to a newspaper grow into a national campaign for children's libraries?
"Join our campaign for cleaner beaches," urged the poster in the community centre window.
I would gladly join a campaign to save the town's last cinema from demolition.
The advertising campaign used a single photograph and eleven carefully chosen words.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - m - p - a - i - g - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the learner to plan a short campaign for a cause they care about, naming its goal and its first action. Their answer should use the word 'campaign' at least twice.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something clear or easier to understand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Can you clarify what you mean by that?
practiceSentences: Can you clarify what you mean by that?
A good storyteller pauses to clarify who is speaking before the plot races ahead.
Could you clarify whether this legend comes from the coast or from the highlands?
"Let me clarify my point," said the editor, "because the summary blurred two separate ideas."
I asked the poet to clarify her final image, and her answer changed how I read the whole poem.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - a - r - i - f - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'clarify', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To attack someone or something violently or with strong words.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The pressure assailed everyone during finals week.
practiceSentences: The pressure assailed everyone during finals week.
Doubts began to assail the young author on the night before her book launch.
Why do critics assail every retelling of the epic instead of judging each on its merits?
"Questions will assail you after the screening," warned the director, "so prepare honest answers."
Worries used to assail me before performances until I learned to focus on the first line only.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - s - s - a - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'assail', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that has no limits or end; it goes on forever.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The universe is so big that it seems infinite.
practiceSentences: The universe is so big that it seems infinite.
A skilled narrator can conjure infinite worlds from a handful of ordinary words.
Is the library of possible stories truly infinite, or do all tales share a few ancient roots?
"Children have infinite patience for a good story," smiled the puppeteer, "and none for a dull one."
Staring at the desert sky, I felt the infinite scale of the universe press gently on my imagination.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - f - i - n - i - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'infinite', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make someone want to do something or work harder.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher tried to motivate the students by giving them fun projects.
practiceSentences: The teacher tried to motivate the students by giving them fun projects.
Folk tales often motivate listeners by showing ordinary people outwitting powerful tyrants.
What could motivate a busy farmer to walk ten miles just to hear a travelling storyteller?
"Deadlines motivate me far more than inspiration does," confessed the novelist during the interview.
Reading about young inventors motivates me to finish my projects instead of merely planning them.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - t - i - v - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'motivate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The ability to keep going and not get tired easily.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Running every day helped her build her stamina for the marathon.
practiceSentences: Running every day helped her build her stamina for the marathon.
Reciting an entire epic from memory demands stamina as well as artistry.
How do the interpreters maintain their stamina during festivals that run late into the night?
"Build your stamina with short daily practices," the drama teacher advised the new cast.
Training for the relay taught me that stamina grows quietly, one unremarkable session at a time.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - a - m - i - n - a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'stamina', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Strange or mysterious, often in a way that is unsettling.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She had an uncanny ability to guess what people were thinking.
practiceSentences: She had an uncanny ability to guess what people were thinking.
The twins share an uncanny ability to finish each other's stories without missing a beat.
Isn't it uncanny how folk tales from distant continents echo the very same warnings?
"Her impression of the headmaster is uncanny," whispered Theo, trying hard not to laugh.
I felt an uncanny sense of recognition in the old city, as if my grandmother's stories had mapped it for me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - n - c - a - n - n - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'uncanny', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To be full of or to have a lot of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The garden was teeming with colourful flowers in the spring.
practiceSentences: The garden was teeming with colourful flowers in the spring.
The bazaar seemed to teem with stories, each stall holder eager to share one.
Why do the world's oldest myths teem with rivers, serpents, and impossible journeys?
"These pages teem with life," wrote the reviewer, "from the first line to the last."

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'teem', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Connected to the topic or situation being discussed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The teacher said our homework was relevant to what we were learning.
practiceSentences: The teacher said our homework was relevant to what we were learning.
A century later, the fable's warning about greed remains painfully relevant.
How do you keep an ancient legend relevant for listeners raised on video games?
"Cut anything that is not relevant," said the editor, "however beautiful the sentence may be."
I opened my speech with a relevant proverb, and the restless audience settled immediately.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - l - e - v - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'relevant', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Able to be shaped or bent easily without breaking.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The metal is malleable, so it can be formed into different shapes.
practiceSentences: The metal is malleable, so it can be formed into different shapes.

Why is memory so malleable that two witnesses can honestly describe different events?

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - l - l - e - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'malleable', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very eager or enthusiastic about something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She is an avid reader who finishes a book every week.
practiceSentences: She is an avid reader who finishes a book every week.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'avid', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Taking unnecessary risks without thinking about consequences.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It was foolhardy of him to climb without safety gear.
practiceSentences: It was foolhardy of him to climb without safety gear.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'foolhardy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Global Storytelling and Creativity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stick out or extend beyond something else.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The rocks jut out from the cliff, making it look dramatic.
practiceSentences: The rocks jut out from the cliff, making it look dramatic.

practiceSentences: The dark clouds looked ominous, making us worry about the storm.

practiceSentences: We enjoyed a balmy evening at the beach.

readings.title: Global Storytelling and Creativity: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 8 of your Year 8 English course. This unit is called Global Storytelling and Creativity, and it is one of the most exciting units you will explore this year. Throughout this unit, you will discover how stories shape the world around us, how creative people use language to inspire and persuade, and how you can develop your own storytelling skills. You will read a powerful original story called The Edge of the Infinite, which follows a young Somali storyteller named Hannah as she competes in a national youth storytelling competition in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania. Her journey will show you that great stories do not simply entertain people; they change lives, preserve memories, and give voice to communities that might otherwise be forgotten.
In this unit, you will also focus on a range of important grammar structures. Your primary grammar focus is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, which allows you to talk about actions that started in the past and continue into the present. You will also study reported speech, which helps you tell others what someone has said without using their exact words. Additionally, you will practise using prepositions with nouns, understand the difference between the past perfect and past simple, learn about adjective plus infinitive structures, explore comparative and superlative forms, and work on adverb placement in the present perfect tense.
Storytelling is deeply woven into East African culture. From the oral traditions of Somali poetry and the Swahili coast to the modern digital narratives being created by young people across the region, stories have always been a powerful way to share knowledge, preserve history, and build understanding between communities. This unit invites you to see yourself as part of that tradition. By the end of this unit, you will have the vocabulary, grammar, and confidence to tell your own stories in English with clarity, creativity, and purpose.
Because this is a self-paced course, you can work through each section at your own speed. Take time to complete every activity, revisit sections you find challenging, and make use of the AI tutor whenever you need help. Your teacher will meet with you twice a week to discuss your progress, answer questions, and help you practise speaking. Between those sessions, this lesson is your main guide.
By the end of this unit, you should be able to:
Use the Present Perfect Continuous tense correctly in spoken and written English to describe ongoing actions.
Convert direct speech into reported speech, adjusting pronouns, verb tenses, and time expressions appropriately.
Combine prepositions with nouns to create clear and natural-sounding prepositional phrases.
Distinguish between the past perfect and past simple and use each correctly when narrating past events.
Form adjective plus infinitive structures to express feelings and opinions about actions.
Apply comparative and superlative structures when comparing people, places, and things.
Place adverbs correctly in present perfect sentences.
Use all thirty-three vocabulary words from this unit confidently in your own sentences.
Read and understand the story The Edge of the Infinite and discuss its themes of perseverance, storytelling, and memory.
Write an original short story or creative narrative that demonstrates your understanding of this unit's grammar and vocabulary.
Speak confidently about topics related to global storytelling, creativity, and the power of narrative.
Reflect on your own learning and identify areas for further improvement.
Before you begin the main content of this unit, it is important to familiarise yourself with the key vocabulary words you will encounter. This unit contains thirty-three carefully selected words that connect to the themes of storytelling, creativity, perseverance, and community. Many of these words will appear in the story, grammar exercises, and writing activities throughout the unit.
Take a few minutes to read through the list below. You do not need to memorise every word right now. Instead, try to get a general sense of what each word means. As you work through the unit, you will encounter these words in context, which will help you understand and remember them more deeply.

readings.title: Global Storytelling and Creativity: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
As you read through the story and complete the activities in this unit, pay close attention to how these words are used in context. Seeing a word in a real sentence helps you remember it far better than simply reading a definition. Try to use at least five new words each day in your own conversations or writing practice.
Grammar is the framework that holds your English together. Without it, your ideas might be clear in your mind, but they will not come across clearly to your reader or listener. In this unit, you will focus on several important grammar structures. Your main grammar focus is the Present Perfect Continuous tense, but you will also practise reported speech, prepositions with nouns, past perfect versus past simple, adjective plus infinitive constructions, comparative and superlative structures, and adverb placement.
The Present Perfect Continuous tense is used to describe actions that started in the past and are still happening now, or actions that recently stopped but have a visible result in the present. This tense is formed using the structure: subject + has/have + been + verb-ing. It creates a sense of duration and continuity that connects the past to the present moment.
For example, consider the sentence: She has been writing stories since she was ten. This tells us that the action of writing started in the past (when she was ten) and continues up to the present. The action is not finished. Compare this with the simple past tense: She wrote stories when she was ten. This tells us that the action happened in the past and is now complete. The Present Perfect Continuous emphasises that the action is ongoing.
Here are the key rules for forming this tense:
Use have been with I, you, we, and they: I have been studying / They have been practising.
Use has been with he, she, and it: He has been running / She has been reading.
To make it negative, add not after have or has: I have not been sleeping well / She has not been eating properly.
This tense is especially useful in storytelling because it allows you to show that characters have been engaged in activities over a period of time. In the story you will read, you will see sentences like Hannah had been writing since she was ten and The students had been preparing for months. These sentences create a sense of dedication and effort that makes the narrative more compelling.
Reported speech is how you tell someone what another person said, without quoting their exact words. This is essential for storytelling because narrators frequently report what characters have said. When you convert direct speech to reported speech, you usually need to change the pronouns, shift the verb tense back by one step, and adjust time expressions.
For example: Direct speech: "I love this story," she said. Reported speech: She said she loved that story. Notice how I changed to she, love changed to loved, and this changed to that. These shifts happen because you are moving the speech from the speaker's perspective to the narrator's perspective, and often from the present into the past.
Use said when you are simply reporting what someone expressed. Use told when you mention who was being spoken to. For questions, change the word order and remove the question mark: "Are you coming?" becomes She asked if I was coming. For commands or requests, use the infinitive: "Please help me" becomes He asked me to help him.
Prepositions are small but powerful words like in, at, on, during, and for that show relationships between nouns and other parts of a sentence. When combined with nouns, they create prepositional phrases that tell us where, when, or how something happens. For example: in a commotion, at the scene, during the campaign. Learning which prepositions go with which nouns is essential for sounding natural in English, and the best way to learn these combinations is through reading and practice.

readings.title: Year 8 - Unit 8 Story, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Theme: Passion, perseverance, and the power of storytelling
Setting: A semi-futuristic East African media academy in Dar es Salaam
Genre: Contemporary speculative fiction with emotional and persuasive arcs
Every year, the coastal city of Dar es Salaam teemed with talent, ideas, and noise during the National Youth StoryForge. But this year's competition was not like any before.
It was not just about performance. This was the first time the Ministry of Public Memory had merged the contest with the National Change campaign, a new initiative using stories to spotlight climate, history, and hidden voices across Tanzania.
At the Mambo Media Academy, students had been preparing for months. It was a campaign of ideas, a passionate campaign to bring forgotten truths to the stage.
For twelve weeks, they had trained under mentors, developing endurance and style. The stakes were high: the winners would be broadcast on the national grid, their words archived in a cultural capsule for generations.
Hannah Ibrahim, a seventeen-year-old with an avid curiosity and endless stamina, had been writing since she was ten. She kept her stories hidden until one teacher, Mr. Noah, discovered her notebook of anecdotes and pushed her to audition.
Now, she stood at the edge of the outdoor amphitheatre, her heart pounding. Her breath came fast as a balmy ocean breeze rolled through, lifting the scent of roasted maize and warming plastic chairs.
Inside the preparation tent, the air was thick with commotion — last-minute lines being rehearsed, costumes being adjusted, and an explosion of jargon from students critiquing one another's arcs.
"You need to clarify the second act!" someone barked.
"Your metaphor's too malleable — what does the fire actually represent?"
Hannah kept to herself, sipping ginger tea. She had written a piece called The Flood That Forgot No One, a tale about a girl who learns to speak the ocean's language after her island sinks.
"You're on in three," said a voice. It was Nadia Adam, her mentor — sharp-eyed, calm.
Hannah swallowed. "What if it's not... relevant enough?"
Amal raised an eyebrow. "You've been crusading for three months. This story is more relevant than most speeches I've heard in parliament."
Out on stage, another performer finished a vivid retelling of a market fire in Mwanza. His voice had wafted across the audience like smoke, leaving them hushed.
She stepped onto the stage. The sea was behind her. Lights framed her. A single mic waited.
"I have always feared water. Not because it drowns. But because it remembers."
The crowd stilled. She pushed forward.
"My grandmother said the sea had an infinite tongue. That it told every story you tried to bury. She told me, when I was five, about the day our island cracked."
As she spoke, Hannah's voice grew stronger. She had been writing the piece for weeks, editing at dawn, rehearsing after lights out. Every word came from her bones.
"I saw bodies float past me. I saw boats become coffins. I saw truth jut from the waves like forgotten bones."
Hannah told of the silence after the flood. Of the scarcity of memory. Of how even officials had denied it.
"'It didn't happen,' they said. But we knew. We had dreams of drowning every night."
"And when you deny the past, you abet the next flood."
Then applause — slow, then swelling.
Backstage, Nadia pulled her into a hug.
"I feel like I've just... returned from a war," Hannah said.
Amal grinned. "That's how you know it was real."
The next day, the results were posted. Hannah had made it into the final ten.
She walked into the common room, where the other students were buzzing.
"Girl, that line about the boats?" said another finalist, Nadia. "That engrossed everyone. I stopped breathing."
Someone else muttered, "They'll never let her win, though. Too heavy."
Finals week was even tougher. The pressure assailed everyone. One student fainted. Two others dropped out. Hannah had to recuperate after a night of nausea.

readings.title: Year 8 - Unit 8 Story, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"You've got the stamina. Now use it wisely. Speak not just to perform — but to change."
They refined the ending. Smoothed transitions. Sharpened pauses. Hannah's script now ended with a direct challenge:
"What stories will you tell when the waters rise?"
The night of the final broadcast arrived. Lights lit the open-air venue. Drones hovered above, filming.
Ministers sat in the front row. Screens flickered in cafes across the country.
Hannah wore a simple white robe. No props. Just her voice.
She began again, this time slower. Louder.
She told the same tale — but fuller. She added moments of humour, antics shared with her cousins before the storm. She described her uncle's foolhardy decision to stay behind.
"He said, 'I've lived by the sea, I'll die by the sea.' And the sea replied, 'Then let me take you.'"
She ended again with the same line:
"What stories will you tell when the waters rise?"
Backstage, a minister approached. "That was... necessary," she said. "And brave."
Later, a judge read the results.
A week later, at the post-competition gala, she stood beside Nadia on a rooftop.
"You know," said Nadia, "when I met you, you seemed unsure. Soft."
Hannah looked at the stars. "What do I do now?"
"Now?" Nadia said. "You rest. Then you keep going. The campaign isn't over. It's just changed forms."
Chapter 8: The Edge of the Infinite
Weeks passed. Hannah began receiving letters from schools, climate groups, and even one from a remote village council in Zanzibar. They had heard her story. They wanted her to come speak. To listen. To write new ones.
She travelled to places where the ocean bit the land. Where houses had vanished into saltwater. Where memory had ebbed with the tide.
At each stop, she gathered new voices. She asked old women to tell their stories, to record anecdotes into her tablet. She listened to children who explained floods with drawings.
And at each place, she asked:
"What will you tell when the waters come again?"
Eventually, she returned to Dar es Salaam. But she was not the same girl who had once worried about metaphors and stage fright.
Now, she was working on a full series — a documentary mixed with narrative. She had partnered with engineers and artists, building stories from both science and soul.
Her work was called Feasible Futures. It aimed to show not just loss, but resilience. Solutions. Ways forward.
Because it was feasible to support the planet. It was foolish not to.
And Hannah, who had feared her words might vanish, now knew better.
They would waft into corners she had never seen.
Campaign, clarify, assail, infinite, motivate, stamina, uncanny, teem, relevant, malleable, avid, foolhardy, jut, ominous, balmy, decree, waft, recuperate, innate, counter, antics, commotion, subordinate, affluent, support, anecdote, scarcity, feasible, abet, engross, jargon
Present Perfect Continuous - e.g. had been preparing, had been writing
Reported Speech - e.g. She told me, when I was five, about the day our island cracked
Prepositions + Nouns - e.g. at the edge, in the back, during the campaign
Past Perfect vs. Past Simple - e.g. had trained / stood, had written / began
Adjective + Infinitive - implied in feasible to support
Comparative/Superlative - e.g. more relevant than most speeches
Adverb Placement - e.g. had already left, had just returned

grammar.title: How to Form It
grammar.explanation:
The structure is: Subject + has/have + been + verb-ing
Use have been with I, you, we, they: I have been studying.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The structure is: Subject + has/have + been + verb-ing
Use have been with I, you, we, they: I have been studying.
Use has been with he, she, it: He has been playing soccer.
Negative: Add not after has/have: She has not been studying.
Question: Move has/have before the subject: Have you been eating?
Step-by-Step Guide
Identify the subject: Who is performing the action? (I, you, he, she, it, we, they)
Choose has or have: Use has with he/she/it and have with I/you/we/they.
Add been: This word is always included in the Present Perfect Continuous.
Add the verb with -ing: This is the main action. For example: write becomes writing, prepare becomes preparing.
Add time expressions if needed: Common ones include since, for, all day, all week.
Examples
They have been working on the project since Monday.
I have been learning English for three years.
We have been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes.
He has been playing video games all day.
You have been helping your sister with her homework.
She has been writing since she was ten years old.
The streets have been teeming with visitors since the festival started.
They have been preparing for the competition for three months.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Forgetting "been"
Incorrect: She has working hard.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Forgetting "been"
Incorrect: She has working hard.
Correct: She has been working hard.
Mistake 2: Using the wrong form of "have"
Incorrect: They has been playing.
Correct: They have been playing.
Mistake 3: Forgetting to add "-ing"
Incorrect: I have been play.
Correct: I have been playing.
Memory Tip
Think of the Present Perfect Continuous as a bridge between the past and the present. The formula is: has/have + been + action-ing. This bridge connects what started in the past to what is happening right now.
Practice Exercise
Complete the sentences using the Present Perfect Continuous tense:
She (dance) __________ for two hours.
They (watch) __________ that movie since last night.
I (read) __________ this book all week.
He (train) __________ for the marathon for months.
We (wait) __________ for you since morning.
The wind (blow) __________ strongly all afternoon.
You (practise) __________ your speech for days.
It (rain) __________ since we arrived.
✅ Answer Key
1. has been dancing
2. have been watching
3. have been reading
4. has been training
5. have been waiting
6. has been blowing
7. have been practising
8. has been raining
2. Reported Speech
What It Is and Why It Matters
Reported speech (also called indirect speech) is how you tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. This is a vital skill in storytelling because narrators frequently need to report what characters have said. It is also essential in everyday communication when you share information from conversations.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form It
grammar.explanation:
When converting direct speech to reported speech, you need to make three key changes:
Change the pronouns to match the new speaker. "I am happy" becomes "he was happy" (I changes to he).
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
When converting direct speech to reported speech, you need to make three key changes:
Change the pronouns to match the new speaker. "I am happy" becomes "he was happy" (I changes to he).
Shift the verb tense back one step. Present Simple becomes Past Simple. Present Continuous becomes Past Continuous. Present Perfect becomes Past Perfect. Will becomes would. Can becomes could.
Adjust time and place expressions. Today becomes that day. Tomorrow becomes the next day. Here becomes there. This becomes that.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form it in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Using "Said" vs. "Told"
grammar.explanation:
Use said when you do not mention the listener: He said he would come.
Use told when you mention who was spoken to: He told me he would come.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use said when you do not mention the listener: He said he would come.
Use told when you mention who was spoken to: He told me he would come.
Reporting Questions
For yes/no questions, use if or whether and change the word order to a statement:
Direct: "Are you coming?" Reported: She asked if I was coming.
Direct: "Have you finished?" Reported: He asked whether I had finished.
For wh-questions, keep the question word but change to statement word order:
Direct: "Where are you going?" Reported: She asked where I was going.
Reporting Commands and Requests
Use to + infinitive for commands and requests:
Direct: "Please help me." Reported: He asked me to help him.
Direct: "Don't be late." Reported: She told me not to be late.
Examples
He said the antics were foolish.
She asked them to clarify their roles.
They said they would arrive by noon.
He told me to finish my homework.
Hannah said she had always feared water.
Amal told her that the campaign was not over.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply using "said" vs. "told" in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Keeping the same verb tense
Incorrect: She said she loves reading.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Keeping the same verb tense
Incorrect: She said she loves reading.
Correct: She said she loved reading. (Present shifts to past.)
Mistake 2: Using direct quotes instead of reported speech
Incorrect: She said "I love reading."
Correct: She said she loved reading.
Memory Tip
Think of reported speech as telling a story about a conversation. When you tell a story, you shift the details to fit your perspective: shift the pronouns, shift the tense, and tell it your way.
Practice Exercise
Transform the following sentences into reported speech:
"I love reading," he said.
"Can you help me?" she asked.
"Please close the door," he requested.
"We have been practising all day," they said.
"I will finish my story tonight," Hannah said.
"Where is the competition?" he asked.
✅ Answer Key
1. He said he loved reading.
2. She asked if I could help her.
3. He asked me to close the door.
4. They said they had been practising all day.
5. Hannah said she would finish her story that night.
6. He asked where the competition was.
3. Prepositions with Nouns
What It Means
A preposition is a word that shows the relationship between a noun and other words in a sentence. Common prepositions include in, at, on, during, for, with, by, and of. When combined with nouns, they create prepositional phrases that describe where, when, or how something happens.

grammar.title: How to Form Prepositional Phrases
grammar.explanation:
Choose a preposition: in, at, on, during, for, with, by, of.
Add a noun: commotion, scene, campaign, counter, etc.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Choose a preposition: in, at, on, during, for, with, by, of.
Add a noun: commotion, scene, campaign, counter, etc.
Use in a sentence: The children were playing in a commotion.
Examples
There was loud talking in a commotion.
The police arrived at the scene quickly.
Many people joined during the campaign.
She waited at the counter for her order.
There was a scarcity of resources in the village.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Story Retelling
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
After reading The Edge of the Infinite, practise retelling the story in your own words. You do not need to memorise every detail. Instead, focus on the main events and try to use at least ten vocabulary words from this unit as you speak. You can practise alone by recording yourself, or you can tell the story to a family member, a friend, or your AI tutor.
When retelling the story, try to use the Present Perfect Continuous tense naturally. For example, instead of saying Hannah prepared for months, you could say Hannah had been preparing for months. This small change adds depth and sophistication to your spoken English.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise story retelling and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Reported Speech Conversations
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner or your AI tutor. One person makes a statement, and the other person reports it. Keep going back and forth, practising the tense shifts and pronoun changes required for reported speech.
Example exchange:
Person A: I have been studying all morning.
Person B reports: She said she had been studying all morning.
Person B: I am excited about the storytelling competition.
Person A reports: He said he was excited about the storytelling competition.
Try to keep the conversation going for at least five minutes. The more you practise, the more natural reported speech will become.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise reported speech conversations and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: My Storytelling campaign
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think about a cause or topic that matters to you. It could be anything: protecting the environment, supporting education in your community, preserving cultural traditions, or standing up against injustice. Prepare a short two-minute speech in which you explain what your campaign is, why it matters, and what you have been doing to support it. Try to use the Present Perfect Continuous tense at least three times in your speech.
Here is a framework to help you organise your speech:
Introduction: Tell your audience what your campaign is and why you care about it.
Background: Explain how you first became interested in this cause. What motivated you?
Actions: Describe what you have been doing to support your cause. Use the Present Perfect Continuous tense here.
Impact: Share any results or changes you have noticed because of your efforts.
Call to action: End by asking your audience to join your campaign or support the cause.
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try saying: Can you listen to my speech and give me feedback on my grammar?
Or: Can you help me practise reported speech by having a conversation with me?
Writing
Writing is where all your learning comes together. In this section, you will complete several writing tasks that help you practise the grammar, vocabulary, and storytelling skills from this unit. Each task builds on the previous one, so work through them in order.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise my storytelling campaign and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

writing.title: Writing 3: Short Story
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write your own short story of 200 to 300 words. Your story should be set in an East African location and feature a young character who faces a challenge related to storytelling, creativity, or self-expression. Your story must include at least five vocabulary words from this unit, at least two examples of the Present Perfect Continuous tense, and at least one example of reported speech.
Before you begin writing, take a few minutes to plan your story. Think about the following elements:
Setting: Where does your story take place? A school, a market, a coastal village, an academy?
Character: Who is your main character? What are they like? What do they want?
Challenge: What obstacle does your character face?
Resolution: How does your character overcome the challenge?
Theme: What message or lesson does your story convey?
Once you have a plan, write your first draft. Do not worry about making it perfect. You can always revise and improve it later. After finishing your first draft, read it aloud to yourself or to your AI tutor. Check that your grammar is correct and that your vocabulary words are used naturally in context.

Activities and Exercises
This section provides a variety of exercises to help you practise and reinforce the grammar and vocabulary you have been learning throughout this unit. Complete each exercise carefully, and check your answers using the answer keys provided. If you get an answer wrong, do not simply move on. Go back to the relevant grammar section, review the rule, and try again.
Exercise 1: Present Perfect Continuous - Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct Present Perfect Continuous form of the verb in brackets.

activities.title: Activities 2: Fill in the Story Gaps
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete the following short passage by filling in the blanks with words from the vocabulary list. Each word is used only once.
The streets of Mombasa were __________ with tourists during the annual cultural festival. There was a great __________ in the main square as performers gathered from across the region. Sarah, an __________ reader and aspiring writer, had been working on her performance for weeks. She knew she needed __________ to get through the long rehearsal schedule. Her mentor told her it was __________ to complete the project if she stayed focused. As evening fell, a __________ breeze carried the scent of spiced tea across the courtyard, and the __________ clouds in the distance hinted at an approaching storm. Despite the challenges, Sarah refused to let anything __________ her concentration from the story she had been crafting.
✅ Answer Key - Fill in the Story Gaps
teeming, commotion, avid, stamina, feasible, balmy, ominous, assail
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Word Families
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Many vocabulary words belong to word families. Understanding related forms helps you use words flexibly. Complete the table below by filling in the missing forms.
✅ Answer Key - Word Families
clarify - clarification - clear / clarified
motivate - motivation - motivated / motivating
recuperate - recuperation - recuperative
support - support / supporter - supportive
engross - engrossment - engrossing / engrossed
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Vocabulary in Context
activities.instructionsAndItems:
For each sentence below, decide whether the underlined vocabulary word is used correctly or incorrectly. If it is incorrect, rewrite the sentence with the correct word or correct usage.
The teacher asked us to clarify our answers by making them more confusing. (Correct or incorrect?)
It was foolhardy of the captain to check the safety equipment before the voyage. (Correct or incorrect?)
The smell of freshly baked bread wafted through the open window. (Correct or incorrect?)
The affluent family struggled to afford basic necessities. (Correct or incorrect?)
She shared a funny anecdote about her first day at school. (Correct or incorrect?)
✅ Answer Key - Vocabulary in Context
1. Incorrect. Clarify means to make something clearer, not more confusing.
2. Incorrect. Foolhardy means reckless, but checking safety equipment is sensible, not reckless.
3. Correct.
4. Incorrect. Affluent means wealthy, so an affluent family would not struggle to afford things.
5. Correct.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A strong effort to achieve something important, often related to a cause or belief. The students started a campaign to raise money for their school library.
To make something clear or easier to understand. Can you clarify what you mean by that?
To attack someone or something violently or with strong words. The pressure assailed everyone during finals week.
Something that has no limits or end; it goes on forever. The universe is so big that it seems infinite.
To make someone want to do something or work harder. The teacher tried to motivate the students by giving them fun projects.
The ability to keep going and not get tired easily. Running every day helped her build her stamina for the marathon.
Strange or mysterious, often in a way that is unsettling. She had an uncanny ability to guess what people were thinking.
To be full of or to have a lot of something. The garden was teeming with colourful flowers in the spring.
Connected to the topic or situation being discussed. The teacher said our homework was relevant to what we were learning.
Able to be shaped or bent easily without breaking. The metal is malleable, so it can be formed into different shapes.
